findings.title: No authentication on PM server API endpoints
findings.description: Tasks 2, 6 expose sensitive API endpoints (GET /api/delegation/status, GET /api/pipeline/status, GET /api/validation/report/{run_id}) on the cto-pm Elysia server with no authentication or authorization mechanism. These endpoints return internal delegation details, Linear user IDs, pipeline state, and issue URLs. Task 7 proposes JWT auth for the frontend but the backend endpoints themselves have no auth middleware defined in Tasks 2 or 6. Anyone with network access to the cto-pm service can query all pipeline and delegation data.
findings.recommendation: Add authentication middleware to all /api/* routes on the Elysia server as part of Task 2. At minimum, require a Bearer token validated against a shared secret or JWT. Define this as a subtask before the dashboard (Task 7) so the API is secured regardless of whether the frontend is deployed. The auth endpoint referenced in Task 7 (POST /api/auth/login) should be implemented in Task 2 scope.

findings.title: RBAC and ServiceAccount deferred to stretch task (Task 10)
findings.description: The cto-pm pods in Task 1 run with the default ServiceAccount which typically has broader permissions than needed. Dedicated RBAC (ServiceAccount, Role, RoleBinding with least-privilege) is deferred entirely to Task 10, which depends on Task 9, which depends on Task 8. This means the entire core pipeline (Tasks 1-8) runs without any Kubernetes RBAC scoping. The secret-validation-job (subtask 1006) even creates its own ad-hoc ServiceAccount and RoleBinding for secret read access, but no similar consideration is given to the main application pods.
findings.recommendation: Move ServiceAccount creation and basic Role/RoleBinding from Task 10 into Task 1 as a subtask. At minimum, create the sigma-1-pipeline-sa with read-only ConfigMap/Secret access and assign it to cto-pm pods from the start. The full RBAC audit and ClusterRole considerations can remain in Task 10.

findings.title: GitHub PAT stored as ExternalSecret with broad scope implications
findings.description: The sigma-1-github-token ExternalSecret (subtask 1005) stores a GitHub Personal Access Token. The task does not specify the required token scopes, and there is no guidance on using a fine-grained PAT scoped to the 5dlabs/sigma-1 repository only. A classic PAT with repo scope grants access to ALL repositories the user can access. Additionally, the token is consumed by multiple workloads (PR creation in Task 4, E2E tests in Task 8) with no separation of credentials.
findings.recommendation: Document the required minimum GitHub token scopes (contents:write, pull_requests:write scoped to 5dlabs/sigma-1 only). Use a fine-grained PAT or GitHub App installation token rather than a classic PAT. Consider separate tokens for CI/test vs. pipeline operations so that test token compromise has limited blast radius.

findings.title: Linear API token shared across pipeline and test workloads
findings.description: The sigma-1-linear-token ExternalSecret is consumed by cto-pm for production pipeline operations and by E2E tests (Task 8) for test issue creation, verification, and cleanup. A single token used for both purposes means test code has the same write access as production code. If E2E tests have a bug, they could modify production Linear issues.
findings.recommendation: Use a separate Linear API token for E2E testing that is scoped to a test team/project. Store it as a separate ExternalSecret (e.g., sigma-1-linear-test-token). The E2E test adapter should use this test token, not the production pipeline token.

findings.title: No input validation on pipeline intake endpoint
findings.description: Task 6 and Task 8 reference triggering the pipeline via POST /api/pipeline/intake, but no task defines input validation, schema validation, or sanitization for the PRD payload. The intake endpoint accepts arbitrary content that flows through deliberation, task generation, and into Linear issue creation and GitHub PR content. Malicious or malformed PRD input could lead to injection into Linear issues (stored XSS in issue descriptions), GitHub PR content, or Discord notifications.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit input validation as a subtask in Task 2 or Task 6. Define a PRD schema using Zod or TypeBox (both compatible with Elysia). Validate and sanitize all user-provided content before it flows into Linear API calls, GitHub file content, or Discord payloads. Specifically sanitize markdown content to prevent HTML injection in Linear/GitHub.

findings.title: No rate limiting on PM server API endpoints
findings.description: The cto-pm Elysia server exposes multiple API endpoints (delegation status, pipeline status, validation report, pipeline intake) with no rate limiting defined. Rate limiting is only mentioned for the Ingress in the stretch Task 9 (subtask 9003). Until Task 9 is deployed, the API is unprotected against abuse or accidental tight-loop polling.
findings.recommendation: Add application-level rate limiting to the Elysia server in Task 2 using Elysia's rate-limit plugin or a simple token-bucket middleware. This provides defense-in-depth even after Ingress rate limiting is added in Task 9. The pipeline intake endpoint should be especially restricted (e.g., 5 req/min).

findings.title: Network policies deferred to stretch task (Task 9)
findings.description: Default-deny NetworkPolicies for the sigma-1 namespace are entirely deferred to Task 9 (subtask 9004-9006), which depends on Task 8. This means throughout the entire development and validation phase (Tasks 1-8), the sigma-1 namespace has unrestricted network access. Any compromised pod can communicate freely with all other namespaces and external endpoints.
findings.recommendation: Apply a basic default-deny ingress NetworkPolicy in Task 1 with explicit allow rules for known traffic patterns. Egress can be left open initially but document the intended egress restrictions. Full egress lockdown can remain in Task 9, but having ingress deny from day one significantly reduces attack surface.

findings.title: Container images not pinned to digest and no scanning in core tasks
findings.description: The secret-validation-job (subtask 1006) uses bitnami/kubectl, health-check-job (subtask 1008) uses curlimages/curl, and the security scan CronJob (subtask 10008) uses aquasec/trivy:latest — all referenced by tag rather than digest. Additionally, container image scanning is deferred to Task 10 as a stretch goal. The core pipeline tasks (1-8) have no image scanning gate.
findings.recommendation: Pin all container image references to SHA256 digests in the Kubernetes manifests (e.g., bitnami/kubectl@sha256:...). Add a subtask to Task 1 that documents approved base images and their digests. Move the Trivy scanning CronJob (or a CI-triggered scan) to run before Task 8 E2E tests rather than as a post-deployment stretch task.

findings.title: JWT implementation in Task 7 has security design gaps
findings.description: The dashboard JWT auth (subtask 7003-7004) decodes the JWT in middleware without full verification ('Decode the JWT without full verification — that's the server's job'). While middleware may only need to check expiry for routing, this means forged tokens with future exp claims would pass middleware and reach the Next.js API proxy routes. The proxy routes forward requests to PM server, which itself has no auth (per SEC-001). Additionally, the refresh mechanism design is unresolved (decision point), and storing credentials for silent re-authentication is explicitly listed as an option, which would be a significant security anti-pattern.
findings.recommendation: Verify JWT signature in Next.js middleware using the server's public key or shared secret — do not skip verification. Implement proper refresh tokens (separate from access tokens) with rotation on use. Never store user credentials on the client for silent re-authentication. Ensure the PM server validates JWTs independently on every request.

findings.title: Discord webhook URL treated as a secret but webhook URLs are bearer credentials
findings.description: The Discord webhook URL (subtask 1003) is stored as an ExternalSecret, which is appropriate. However, the webhook URL is a bearer credential — anyone with the URL can post to the Discord channel. The URL is referenced in the ConfigMap sigma-1-infra-endpoints as DISCORD_BRIDGE_URL, but this points to the in-cluster bridge service, not the raw webhook. Confirm the raw webhook URL is never exposed in ConfigMaps, logs, or error messages.
findings.recommendation: Verify that the raw Discord webhook URL from the ExternalSecret is only consumed by the discord-bridge service and never propagated to application-level ConfigMaps, environment variables, or logs. The sigma-1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap correctly references the bridge service URL, not the webhook — maintain this separation.

findings.title: Audit logging deferred to stretch tasks with no interim logging standards
findings.description: Comprehensive audit logging (Kubernetes-level and application-level) is deferred to Task 10 subtasks 10005-10007. While Task 2 and Task 3 add structured logging, there is no explicit security audit trail for the core pipeline. Events like 'who triggered the pipeline', 'which issues were created with which credentials', and 'which PRs were opened' are logged ad-hoc rather than in a formal audit schema.
findings.recommendation: Define a minimal audit log schema in Task 2 that covers security-relevant events: pipeline trigger source, credential usage (which ExternalSecret was accessed, not the value), issue creation, PR creation, and notification dispatch. This schema can be expanded in Task 10 but the baseline should exist from the start.

findings.title: Secret validation job logs may expose secret metadata
findings.description: The secret-validation-job (subtask 1006) reads secret data fields and checks they are non-empty. While it does not log the secret values, it logs which secrets are populated or missing. If extended carelessly, it could leak secret values. The job also requires a ServiceAccount with get/list on secrets, which is a privileged operation.
findings.recommendation: Ensure the validation script only checks for the existence and non-emptiness of data keys — never log the decoded values or base64 content. Add a comment in the script explicitly warning against logging secret values. The ServiceAccount for this job should be scoped to sigma-1 namespace only and deleted or disabled after initial validation if it's a one-shot operation.

findings.title: No dependency auditing task in the plan
findings.description: The expanded tasks do not include a step for auditing npm/Bun dependencies for known vulnerabilities. The cto-pm Bun/Elysia service and the Next.js dashboard both pull npm packages but there is no bun audit, npm audit, or Snyk/Dependabot integration mentioned.
findings.recommendation: Add a subtask to Task 2 (or as a shared CI task) that runs bun audit (or equivalent) and fails on critical vulnerabilities. For the Next.js dashboard (Task 7), include npm audit in the build pipeline. Consider enabling Dependabot or Renovate on the sigma-1 repository.

findings.title: NOUS_API_KEY sent to external API without TLS verification guarantee
findings.description: The NousProvider (subtask 3003) calls an external API with the NOUS_API_KEY as a Bearer token. While the URL defaults to https://, there is no explicit check that TLS certificate verification is enabled in Bun's fetch implementation. If TLS verification is accidentally disabled (e.g., NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 in the environment), the API key could be intercepted via MITM.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly verify that Bun's fetch does not have TLS verification disabled. Add an assertion or startup check that NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED is not set to 0. Document that all external API calls must use verified TLS connections.

findings.title: E2E test cleanup uses credentials with delete permissions on production resources
findings.description: Task 8 E2E tests create real Linear issues, GitHub PRs, and then clean them up (subtask 8011). The cleanup operations require delete/close permissions on Linear issues and GitHub PRs. If the same token is used for both production and test operations (per SEC-004), a bug in the cleanup logic could close or archive production pipeline artifacts. Additionally, the test uses the pipeline intake endpoint which triggers real side effects.
findings.recommendation: Isolate E2E test resources using unique prefixes or a dedicated test workspace. Use a separate Linear team/project for test issues. Use a test branch prefix (e.g., test/e2e-*) for GitHub PRs with branch protection rules preventing test branches from merging. Implement a safety check in cleanup that refuses to archive issues or close PRs that don't match the test run's unique identifiers.

strengths: ExternalSecrets operator is used for all secret management — no hardcoded secrets in manifests or code
NOUS_API_KEY is explicitly marked as optional with graceful degradation, preventing pipeline failures from missing optional credentials
The NousProvider implementation (subtask 3003) explicitly redacts API keys in log output with [REDACTED]
The three-tier research provider pattern (Hermes → NOUS → Skip) implements proper circuit-breaker-style error isolation
Discord webhook URL is properly separated — the ConfigMap references the in-cluster bridge service URL, not the raw webhook credential
Notification failures are explicitly designed to never block the pipeline (graceful degradation pattern throughout)
The PR creation flow (Task 4) includes explicit error handling for 403/404 with diagnostic messages about token permissions
The plan uses ExternalSecrets with refreshInterval for secret rotation rather than static Kubernetes Secrets
All resources are labeled for traceability (sigma-1-pipeline: infra/production) enabling targeted cleanup and auditing
The plan explicitly separates required vs optional secrets in the validation job, preventing pipeline failures from optional dependencies
summary: The task plan has a strong architecture for graceful degradation and secret management via ExternalSecrets, but has a high overall risk due to several critical gaps in the core pipeline (Tasks 1-8): no authentication on PM server API endpoints, no input validation on the pipeline intake, RBAC deferred entirely to stretch tasks, and shared credentials between production and test workloads. The most impactful remediation would be adding API authentication and basic RBAC to Task 1-2 scope rather than deferring all security hardening to Tasks 9-10, which may never be prioritized. The E2E test suite (Task 8) also poses risk by operating on production resources with the same credentials as the pipeline itself.
